questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with a 1-week history of fever, sore throat, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash on his trunk. He had unprotected sexual intercourse with a new partner 3 weeks ago. He has no significant past medical history. Examination reveals cervical lymphadenopathy and mild hepatosplenomegaly. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of fever, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, rash, and hepatosplenomegaly following a high-risk exposure 3 weeks ago is classic for acute HIV seroconversion. Infectious mononucleosis typically lacks a rash, and secondary syphilis classically involves palmar-plantar rash. CMV and toxoplasmosis are less acute and rarely present with this triad.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old asymptomatic woman attends a sexual health clinic requesting an HIV test. She has no symptoms and her partner recently tested positive for HIV. She is not on any medication. Examination is unremarkable. Her HIV antibody test is positive and confirmatory test confirms infection. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Established HIV infection with normal CD4 count
questions.explanation: An asymptomatic individual with a positive HIV test and no history of acute illness most likely has established HIV infection with preserved immune function. Recent seroconversion without symptoms is possible but less likely in the absence of any preceding illness. Acute infection usually presents with symptoms. HIV-2 is rare in the UK, and false positives are uncommon with confirmatory testing.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known HIV (CD4 count 90 cells/μL) presents with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnoea, dry cough, and low-grade fever. He is not on antiretroviral therapy. On examination, he is tachypnoeic with bilateral fine crackles. Chest X-ray shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The subacute onset, CD4 count below 100 cells/μL, dry cough, and bilateral interstitial infiltrates are highly suggestive of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP). Bacterial pneumonia typically has a more acute onset and focal consolidation. TB often has apical involvement, CMV pneumonitis is rarer, and LIP is associated with HIV in children.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man from sub-Saharan Africa, recently diagnosed with HIV (CD4 count 120 cells/μL), presents with a 3-week history of fever, night sweats, weight loss, and abdominal pain. He has no respiratory symptoms. Examination reveals hepatosplenomegaly and shifting dullness. Chest X-ray is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In a patient with advanced HIV from a TB-endemic area, fever, night sweats, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, and ascites with a normal chest X-ray are typical of extrapulmonary (disseminated) tuberculosis. MAC usually occurs with CD4 <50 and often presents with diarrhoea and anaemia. Lymphoma is possible but less common, and the other options are less likely given the presentation.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with well-controlled HIV (CD4 count 500 cells/μL, on antiretroviral therapy) presents with a 2-month history of purple, non-tender nodules on his lower legs and trunk. He is otherwise well. Examination reveals several 1-2 cm purplish lesions on the shins and abdomen. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Purple, non-tender nodules on the lower extremities and trunk in a patient with HIV, even with high CD4, are classic for Kaposi's sarcoma (caused by HHV-8). Bacillary angiomatosis (Bartonella) typically produces red, papular lesions that bleed easily, and usually occurs with lower CD4 counts. Pyogenic granuloma is solitary and often pedunculated. Melanoma is usually pigmented and irregular. Nodular prurigo is pruritic.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of fever, sore throat, myalgia, and weight loss. He reports unprotected anal intercourse 3 weeks ago. A fourth-generation HIV antigen/antibody test is negative. What is the single most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: In acute HIV infection, the fourth-generation test may be negative during the window period (first 4 weeks). HIV RNA PCR detects viral RNA directly and is the recommended test to confirm acute infection when suspicion is high.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV infection. He feels well. Which single investigation is most important for determining his risk of opportunistic infections?
questions.explanation: CD4 count is the key marker of immune status and stratifies the risk of opportunistic infections; a count <200 cells/µL indicates severe immunodeficiency and defines AIDS.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV (CD4 count 120 cells/µL) presents with a 2-week history of dry cough, fever, and progressive dyspnoea. CT chest shows bilateral ground-glass opacities. What is the most appropriate diagnostic test to confirm the likely diagnosis?
options: Bronchoalveolar lavage for Pneumocystis jirovecii immunofluorescence
questions.explanation: The patient has typical features of Pneumocystis pneumonia (PCP) given CD4 <200 and ground-glass opacities. Bronchoalveolar lavage with immunofluorescence or PCR is the gold standard for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A healthcare worker sustains a needlestick injury from a patient known to be HIV-positive with an undetectable viral load on ART. What is the single most appropriate investigation?
options: CD4 count of the source patient
questions.explanation: UK guidelines recommend baseline HIV testing for occupational exposures, even when the source has undetectable viral load, to document serostatus and provide a reference for follow-up testing.

questions.stem: A baby is born to a mother with HIV who was on antiretroviral therapy with viral load <50 copies/mL throughout pregnancy. What is the single most appropriate test to determine if the baby has been infected with HIV?
options: HIV antibody test at 12 months of age
questions.explanation: In infants born to HIV-positive mothers, maternal antibodies persist for up to 18 months, so antibody tests are unhelpful. HIV RNA PCR (or DNA PCR) is used to detect the virus directly; it is recommended at birth and at 6 weeks according to UK guidelines.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is newly diagnosed with HIV. He is asymptomatic, CD4 count 550 cells/µL, viral load 30,000 copies/mL. He has normal renal function, no hepatitis B or C co-infection, and is not on any other medications. What is the most appropriate first-line antiretroviral regimen?
questions.explanation: UK BHIVA guidelines recommend an integrase inhibitor (dolutegravir) plus two NRTIs as first-line. Tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) is preferred over tenofovir disoproxil (TDF) due to lower renal and bone toxicity, making TAF/FTC + DTG the optimal regimen.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV and pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) is about to start rifampicin-based TB therapy. His CD4 is 200 cells/µL, viral load 100,000 copies/mL. Which antiretroviral regimen is most appropriate to start concurrently?
questions.explanation: Rifampicin is a potent inducer of CYP3A4, reducing levels of protease inhibitors and most integrase inhibitors. Efavirenz is less affected and is the recommended anchor drug when co-administered with rifampicin, thus efavirenz-based ART is preferred.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with HIV presents at 28 weeks gestation in her first pregnancy. She is ART-naive, CD4 450 cells/µL, viral load 50,000 copies/mL. What is the most appropriate antiretroviral regimen to prevent mother-to-child transmission?
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, UK guidelines recommend an integrase inhibitor (raltegravir) with two NRTIs for rapid viral suppression. Zidovudine monotherapy is insufficient; dolutegravir is safe in the second/third trimester but raltegravir has more extensive safety data; efavirenz is avoided in pregnancy due to potential fetal harm.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known HIV (not on ART, defaulted from care) presents with fever, dry cough, and progressive dyspnoea over 2 weeks. He is hypoxic (SpO2 88% on air). Chest X-ray shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates. CD4 count is 40 cells/µL. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for the likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This is moderate-to-severe PCP. First-line therapy is high-dose co-trimoxazole with adjunctive corticosteroids (prednisolone) for hypoxic patients. The other options are second-line alternatives.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with HIV and chronic hepatitis B (HBsAg positive, HBV DNA 10^6 IU/mL) is starting ART. He has normal renal function. Which antiretroviral regimen is most appropriate to treat both infections?
questions.explanation: Both tenofovir and emtricitabine have activity against HBV. Tenofovir disoproxil (TDF) is preferred over tenofovir alafenamide (TAF) for HBV due to better efficacy data, though TAF is now also used. The regimen must include two active anti-HBV drugs; TDF/FTC plus an integrase inhibitor is standard. Lamivudine alone risks resistance; TAF/FTC with darunavir/ritonavir is appropriate but TDF-based is more established for HBV.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the ED with a rash and lymphadenopathy. He is found to have a positive rapid HIV test. He is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Send confirmatory HIV serology and refer to HIV specialist
Order CD4 count and viral load
questions.explanation: A single positive rapid test requires laboratory confirmation before diagnosis; the immediate next step is to send confirmatory serology and refer to an HIV specialist for further management. Starting ART or prophylaxis is premature without confirmed diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known HIV not on ART presents with fever, dry cough, and dyspnoea. Oxygen saturations are 88% on room air. Chest X-ray shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Send blood cultures and start broad-spectrum antibiotics
questions.explanation: The presentation is classic for PCP; immediate empiric treatment with high-dose co-trimoxazole is urgent. Corticosteroids may be added later if hypoxic, and ART is typically deferred during acute opportunistic infections. Bronchoscopy is not immediately needed.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman at 28 weeks gestation is newly diagnosed with HIV. Her CD4 count is 500 cells/µL. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer to obstetrics for planned caesarean section
questions.explanation: In pregnancy, ART should be started immediately regardless of CD4 count to reduce vertical transmission. Co-trimoxazole is not indicated as CD4 >200. C-section is not decided urgently. Viral load will be checked but starting ART is the priority.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with HIV on a stable ART regimen reports missing several doses over the past month. She has no symptoms. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected adherence issues, the first step is to assess virological control with a viral load test. CD4 is less sensitive to recent non-adherence. Resistance testing is considered only if viral load is detectable. Switching ART without evidence of failure is inappropriate.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV (CD4 350 cells/µL) has a positive interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA) for tuberculosis. He is asymptomatic, with no cough, fever, or weight loss. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
questions.explanation: In HIV with positive IGRA, active TB must be excluded before treating latent infection; a chest X-ray is the immediate step. Starting treatment without excluding active disease risks inadequate therapy and resistance. ART can be started concurrently but not before CXR.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old asymptomatic man presents to the GP after a positive HIV test during a routine sexual health screen. He has no past medical history and is not on any medication. Which baseline investigation result is a red flag requiring urgent further action?
questions.explanation: A positive serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) indicates subclinical cryptococcal infection, which can rapidly progress to meningitis if untreated. This requires an urgent lumbar puncture and preemptive antifungal therapy. A low CD4 count (<200) warrants PCP prophylaxis but is not an immediate 'red flag' for a life-threatening infection like cryptococcemia.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old student presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of fever, sore throat, and headache. He reports multiple sexual partners and has not been tested for HIV. On examination, there is bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy and a maculopapular rash on his trunk. Which single clinical sign raises concern for a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Nuchal rigidity is a specific sign of meningeal irritation, indicating possible meningitis. In the context of acute HIV seroconversion, aseptic meningitis is a known complication. Photophobia is also suggestive, but nuchal rigidity on examination is more definitive and warrants urgent lumbar puncture.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known HIV (CD4 count 150 cells/µL) on antiretroviral therapy presents with a 1-week history of dry cough, progressive dyspnoea, and fever. Chest X-ray shows bilateral interstitial infiltrates. Which clinical finding indicates severe disease requiring immediate escalation of care?
questions.explanation: A PaO₂ <70 mmHg (or oxygen saturation <90%) in suspected Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) defines severe disease and is an indication for adjunctive corticosteroids and hospital admission. LDH is often elevated but not a direct marker of severity requiring specific action; hypoxaemia is the key escalation criterion.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with HIV (recently started on nevirapine-based therapy) presents with fever, a diffuse erythematous rash, and malaise. On examination, she has oral ulcerations and conjunctival injection. Which finding is a red flag for a life-threatening drug reaction?
questions.explanation: Oral ulcerations indicate mucosal involvement, which is a hallmark of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) or toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), a life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction to nevirapine. A generalised rash alone, even with fever, is common but does not alone define SJS/TEN. Immediate drug cessation and specialist review are required.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old primigravida at 36 weeks gestation presents to the antenatal clinic. She was recently diagnosed with HIV but has not yet started antiretroviral therapy. She feels well with no symptoms. Which investigation result would be a red flag for a high risk of vertical transmission and necessitate a planned caesarean section?
questions.explanation: An HIV viral load >1000 copies/mL at 36 weeks is the threshold for recommending elective caesarean section to reduce vertical transmission. A low CD4 count is important for prophylaxis decisions but does not directly dictate mode of delivery; viral load is the critical factor for transmission risk.
